observations.recommendation:
We recommended to the Local Chief Executive in compliance with COA Circular No. 2020-006 and Sections 119, 120, and 124 of Volume I of the NGAS Manual for LGUs, the following measures:
Consider the filling-up of the Accountant I positions to ensure there is sufficient personnel for the formation of the Inventory Committee and the conduct of the physical inventory of all PPE of CGD, following the guidelines and procedures provided in COA Circular No. 2020-006;
Establish an Inventory Committee and direct them to prepare and submit a Physical Inventory Plan (PIP) to the COA. Additionally, require them to provide a reliable RPCPPE in the format prescribed in the Government Accounting Manual (GAM) for LGUs; and
Direct the City Accountant and the Head of the General Services Office to maintain a PPE ledger card and a property card for each PPE account, respectively, while ensuring that coordination and reconciliation of records are regularly undertaken to minimize, if not eliminate, any discrepancies in the reported PPE account balances.

We recommend that the Local Chief Executive instruct the following offices to undertake the specified actions:
For the City Accounting Office:
conduct a comprehensive review of all Property, Plant, and Equipment (PPE) accounts to identify tangible items with acquisition costs below the ₱50,000.00 capitalization threshold;
prepare the necessary Journal Entry Vouchers (JEVs) to charge these identified items—both those acquired in Calendar Year (CY) 2024 onwards and in prior years—to the Prior Period Adjustment account, and close the corresponding accumulated depreciation and accumulated impairment losses in the books of accounts; and
explore measures to address manpower constraints, such as hiring additional personnel, to ensure timely and effective implementation of accounting policies.
For the General Services Office (GSO):
coordinate with the City Accounting Office to ensure the proper use of the new forms and documentation prescribed in COA Circular No. 2024-006 for the recording, issuance, and monitoring of semi-expendable properties, in compliance with the updated capitalization threshold.
For the Human Resource Management Office (HRMO):
assess and facilitate the augmentation of staffing resources for both the City Accounting Office and the GSO to address manpower constraints and recommend actions to the LCE as warranted.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
Immediately constitute the Disposal Committee in compliance with COA-DBM Joint Circular No. 2024-001 and initiate proper disposal of unserviceable properties; and
Instruct the Inventory Committee and the Disposal Committee to comply with the provisions of Section A (1.5) of the circular, ensuring an effective and systematic approach to PPE management and disposal.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the City Accountant to conduct review of the cause of discrepancy by tracing and locating the documents affecting the reciprocal accounts and prepare necessary adjusting entry/ies to clear the reciprocal accounts of possible erroneous entries and/or omissions in recording for the fair presentation of the balances of Due from Other Funds and Due to Other Funds accounts in the Financial Statements.

We reiterated the previous year’s recommendation and the Management agreed to continue sending demand letters to the officers and employees concerned and take appropriate actions to enforce liquidation of the cash advances granted to them.

We recommended and Local Chief Executive agreed in compliance International Public Sector Accounting Standards (IPSAS) 1 the following courses of action:
To instruct the City Accountant in coordination with the City Legal Office to exhaust all possible remedies to collect the receivables from the debtors and employees, even those who are no longer connected with the agency, such as resending of demand letters, deduction of amount due from the retirement benefits of concerned employees, and other legal measures to protect the interest of the government.
To require the City Accountant to expedite the evaluation and reconciliation of all overdue accounts to determine proper disposition and request authority to write-off the accounts, if warranted, to ensure fair presentation of the agency’s receivables in the financial statements as required under IPSAS 1.
To advise the City Accountant to strictly adhere with the guidelines relative to the monitoring and analysis of receivable accounts to ensure that such are collected when they become due and demandable.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to instruct the City Accountant to:
Analyze each account and look into the causes of non-settlement or dormancy of the accounts.  Continuous and thorough evaluation of the validity of recorded payables, particularly those pertaining to previous years, should be done immediately in order to determine the appropriate action and/or necessary adjustments;
Revert the accounts payable that remained outstanding over two years and have no existing valid claims to the unappropriated surplus after securing authority from the Sangguniang Panlungsod for the fair presentation of the accounts payable in the financial statements; and
Set-up and/or record accounts payable only when valid and complete supporting documents are available in adherence to Section 46 of PD No. 1177 and Section 4(6) of PD No. 1445 to establish validity, proprietary and existence.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to require the City Accountant to:
Coordinate with other department heads to gather information on the deficiencies noted by the audit team, including non-financial information deemed necessary for users of the financial statements to understand the city's operations; and
Provide adequate disclosures and present them properly in the Notes to the Financial Statements based on the information given by the relevant Department Heads. If revisions are made, furnish a copy to the Audit Team for compliance.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to the following measures of actions in compliance with Sections 3 & 4 of Presidential Decree (PD) No. 1445:
Instruct the City Accountant to conduct a comprehensive review of all trust fund accounts to identify balances related to completed, abandoned, or lapsed projects and locate corresponding Memoranda of Agreement/Trust Agreements;
Instruct the City Treasurer and concerned departments to coordinate and determine the proper disposition of remaining funds and initiate reversion to the source agency, General Fund, or apply them to related lawful purposes in accordance with COA regulations; and
Establish internal policies for the timely closure of trust accounts and prompt reversion or utilization of residual balances.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to undertake the following specified actions:
Issue an order outlining guidelines for implementing the prescribed net take-home pay for all city employees;
Instruct the City Accountant and the Head of the Human Resource Management Office (HRMO) to review loan accommodations and other payroll deductions for the City employees before approval. This review should ensure that these deductions do not cause the employees’ monthly net take-home pay to fall below the prescribed ceiling of ₱5,000.00; and
Instruct those responsible for payroll preparation to diligently monitor the net take-home pay of city employees to ensure compliance with existing laws.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to require the following actions:
Require the concerned office to provide justification for the failure to register the motor vehicles on time and explain why the individuals responsible should not be held accountable for the payment of surcharges and penalties. If no valid justification is provided, the total amount of ₱10,629.00 should be refunded by the personnel found liable.
Direct the concerned office and the GSO to expedite the repair of remaining three out of six identified motor vehicles, as noted during the audit, to prevent further delays in registration and ensure the effective use of government resources.
Instruct the concerned office and accountable officer to closely monitor the vehicle of registration periods and ensure that motor vehicles are registered or renewed within the time frame set by the LTO to avoid penalties and surcharges.
Mandate the concerned office and accountable officer to immediately report to the GSO any motor vehicles needing repairs to facilitate quick action and prevent delays in registration.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the City Treasurer to undertake the following courses of action, to wit:
Immediately process bonding applications for all unbonded AOs to avoid exposure of government funds to risk of loss or misappropriation pursuant to Section 101 of PD No. 1445 and Section 4.1 of Treasury Circular No. 02-2019 dated April 25, 2019; and
Establish a monitoring system to regularly track the bonding status of all AOs to ensure that all officers handling government funds are properly bonded.

We recommended that, in compliance with Republic Act No. 656 Republic Act No. 656 and COA Circular No. 2018-002 dated May 31, 2018, the Local Chief Executive to:
Instruct the GSO to collaborate with the City Management in creating an Appraisal and Inventory Committee. This committee, in coordination with the GSIS, should promptly complete the appraisal process for all buildings and other insurable properties for PIF preparation. The PIF should be submitted to the Audit Team by April 30 on each year to facilitate the establishment of yearly insurance premiums.
Require the City Budget Officer to propose an additional budget based on the insurance premiums covering all identified insurable properties, ensuring that sufficient appropriations are included in future budgets.
Direct the City Accountant to ensure that the proposed budget allocation for insurable properties in the upcoming years is utilized by regularly coordinating with the GSO to prepare the necessary supporting documents.

We recommended the following measures to the Local Chief Executive:
Create a dedicated Inspectorate Team responsible solely for the inspecting all delivered goods. This team will ensure that the received items comply with the technical specifications, codes, and standards. Members of the team should be selected based on their experience, expertise and commitment to this role.
Ensure that the Head of the General Services Office formally accepts delivered items. The Inspectorate Team will conduct the inspection, and both acceptance and inspection properly documented using the Acceptance and Inspection Report.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed on the following:
The GSO to:
Make every effort to prepare the necessary documentary requirements to secure Transfer Certificates of Title (TCT) for all parcels of land acquired or owned. This will provide primary evidence of ownership for these properties and protect the government's interests from third-party claims; and
Ensure that all lots owned by the City have appropriate Certificates of Title and other proof of ownership.
The City Accountant to;
Review and verify the details of the Land Account to confirm that only properties rightfully owned by the City are recorded in the accounting books;
Present a land account that includes a complete description and aggregates all related costs associated with the specific land account.
The City Assessor, General Services Office, and Accounting Office to reconcile their records relative to the land/lots owned by the City Government.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the following:
The Local Development Council
To review the identified priority development projects and address the reasons for their non-implementation; and
To coordinate immediately with the end-users and the Bids and Awards Committee with respect to the unimplemented projects and programs for evaluation and possible implementation.
The City Accountant, in coordination with the City Engineer, will return the remaining unutilized and idle funds from fully implemented projects to the unappropriated surplus. This will ensure that funds are available for other important programs that promote the general welfare of the community.

We recommended and the City Mayor agreed to instruct the City Accountant to continuously ensure timely remittance of all deducted contributions and withheld taxes to the appropriate agencies, in adherence with the pertinent laws, rules and regulations. This will help prevent accumulation of balances and incurrence of corresponding penalties pursuant to PD No. 1445, RA No. 8291, RA No. 9679, and RA No. 7875.
As regards the prior years’ balances of the Due to BIR/GSIS/HDMF/Philhealth accounts, we suggest the following measures for the City Accountant:
Coordinate with the City Human Resources Management Officer to review the payrolls of JO personnel and the relevant list of remittances to the HDMF, to properly and correctly identify the cause of discrepancies;
Increase efforts to verify and review the transactions and pertinent documents to determine and identify the root causes of the non-moving balances from prior years. Make any necessary adjustments to reflect the correct balances of the Inter-Agency Payable Accounts; and
Collaborate with the City Treasurer to ensure the immediate remittance of any overdue contributions and/or withheld taxes to GSIS/HDMF/PhilHealth/BIR.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Government instruct the GSO to meticulously plan and present detailed costing of all goods to be procured especially when it involves customization/modifications and special accessories to promote a clear and unambiguous terms. The detailed costing is essential in determining liquidated damages in case of delay or incomplete delivery.

We also recommended that in future transactions, inspection as to compliance with specification and completeness of delivery be made by the GSO and payment will only be based on actual deliveries in order to protect the interest of the City.

We recommended that the Management instruct the City Accountant to require the contractors to submit the notarized Omnibus Sworn Statement for the paid transactions in compliance with GPPB Resolution No. 05-2020 and Annex A of GPPB Circular No.01-2020.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management instruct the City Administrator and the City Accountant to monitor strict compliance by the Security Agency with its obligations to provide service firearms and other equipment to the security personnel as well as the documentary requirements in order to protect the interest of the City Government and the security personnel assigned thereat. In addition, require the Security Agency to submit the proof of updated remittances to various statutory obligations and the signed payroll for the year 2020. 

We also recommended that if the Security Agency continuously fail to comply with its contractual obligations, apply the necessary legal actions/penalties accordingly.

We recommended that Management instruct the City Engineering Office to consider the requirements of the Building Code of the Philippines in the preparation of the design and/or civil works of all infrastructure projects in order to promote an easy and safe access by the end users particularly the PWD and SC. 

We also recommended that additional work be made for the installation of railing and tactile markings in conformity with the recommended specifications.

We recommended that Management direct its City Budget Officer, City Engineer and the Head of the Waste Management Division to institute the following courses of action to promote a better and sustainable waste management:

Allocate fund for the repair of the existing MRF in the dumpsite or the construction of a new facility. 
Encourage management to purchase a new rotating drum and shredder.
Assess the excavator if it is still economically repairable. 
Coordinate with the remaining nine barangays for the construction of their respective MRF.  By doing so, the City Government will promote a better and sustainable waste management.

We recommended that Management evaluate the reason/s for the undue damage of the communication equipment and if the Accountable Officers are found to be negligent, institute measures to recover the replacement cost of the same or to return an item of the same make which are serviceable.

We recommended that the City Accountant and the Treasurer develop effective mechanism and coordination to meet the demands of timely reporting and submission of accounts.  This is to avoid undue delays in the conduct of post audit and preparing the required reports in compliance with existing laws, rules and regulations.
observations.reason: Management continue to incur delay in the submission especially during the pandemic and because of the alternate work arrangement of employees.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management carefully study the policies and principles of Ordinance No. 2062-2016 and align therein the City Solid Waste Management Plan for purposes of ensuring its sustainability, viability, effectiveness and relevance in relation to local development  towards efficient and environmentally sound solid waste management system.

In addition, the Management should intensify its efforts on educating the people on the waste segregation at source and the enforcement on the use of Materials Recovery Facility (MRF) in each barangay so that residual waste to be collected by the City Waste Management Division will be drastically reduced.

We further recommended that Management evaluate the provisions of the Joint Venture Agreement (JVA) and ensure that it will not violate the provisions of RA 9003 and the Clean Air Act and will promote the best interest of the City government.
observations.reason: Reiterated in Finding No. 13, page 54.

observations.recommendation:

We further recommended that in future similar transactions, the City Officials should be cautious by verifying any impediments attached to all properties to be acquired to avoid disadvantageous dealings.
observations.reason: Reiterated in Finding No. 17, page 63

observations.recommendation:

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management instruct the City Accountant to:
1. set-up and/or record Accounts Payable only when valid and complete supporting documents are available in adherence to Section 46 of PD No. 1177 and Section 4(6) of PD No. 1145 to establish validity, propriety and existence; and
2.  revert accounts payable that have remained outstanding for more than two years and have no existing valid claims to the unappropriated surplus after securing authority from the Sangguniang Panlungsod for the fair presentation of the accounts payable in the financial statements.

We recommended that Management instruct the City Accountant and the GSO to assess the condition of various PPE of the City and make a formal estimate on the RSA of the identified assets that have impairments and accordingly compute the impairment losses thereof for more reliable presentation of the PPE in the Financial Statements.

We recommended the Management to instruct the City Accountant to conduct review of the cause of discrepancy by tracing and locating the documents affecting the reciprocal accounts and undertake necessary adjusting entry/ies to clear the reciprocal accounts of possible erroneous entries and/or omissions in recording for the correct and reliable balances of Due from Other Funds and Due to Other Funds accounts in the Financial Statements.

We recommended and Management agreed to constitute an Inspectorate Team or the Project Monitoring Committee (PMC) who will undertake preliminary inspections on all infrastructure projects of the City to ensure that the progress of the work is properly monitored and any deficiencies will be addressed in a timely manner.

We recommended Management require the City Treasurer’s Office to:

a.	submit written authority from the DILG; 

b.	submit activity reports such as the action plan on the learnings/experiences made from the two LGUs visited to be able to justify the benchmarking activity and avoid disallowance in audit; and 

c.	adhere strictly on the provisions of JMC No. 02 in the conduct of study tour or “lakbay aral” activities of the City.

We recommended that Management submit the lacking documentary requirements to support the liquidation of cash advance and a written explanation why the Choir was not originally included as a participant in the attached Programme for the event to establish the validity and propriety of the transaction.

We recommended and Management agreed to require the City Accountant to ensure that withholding taxes are deducted from payments of honoraria and other similar income payments by the City in compliance with the above-mentioned BIR Revenue Regulations and the same should be remitted to the BIR within the prescribed period, to help and contribute in timely collection of taxes for the government.

We further recommended that the City Development Council, with the assistance of the City Planning and Development Coordinator reassess the necessity of pursuing the unimplemented projects, otherwise, realign or reprogram such projects and programs to more needed developmental projects in conformity with existing laws and in order to uplift the economic and social condition of its constituents.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Treasurer prepare the daily RCI based on the chronological sequence of checks including cancelled and spoiled checks; and eventually record the check disbursement sequentially in the same manner in the Cashbook-Cash in Bank.  The City Accountant shall also the record the disbursement based on the sequence of checks prepared as indicated in the RCI; purposely to reflect the correct balance of Cash in Bank and Expense account.

We have recommended that the approved amount of bond of Disbursing and Liquidating Officer should be increased sufficient to cover the actual maximum cash accountability for a given period in accordance with the revised schedule of bond provided by the Bureau of Treasury primarily to protect the coffers of the city.

We have recommended that the City Treasurer include checks for all bank accounts maintained by the City in the RAAF and submit the same to the Office of the Auditor as prescribed in Section 59 of the LTOM and Section 50 of the Manual of NGAS to ensure that all checks are properly controlled and monitored as to requisition and issuance.  Furthermore, the issuance of checks must be reported chronologically in the RCI that includes cancelled or voided checks. The checks that have been cancelled or voided shall also be disclosed in the RAAF.  Finally, the management must exert effort to locate the missing disbursement voucher and its supporting documents under Check No. 745833 paid to National Food Authority to avoid future suspension or disallowance in audit.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive should cause the withholding of salaries and other amounts due the concerned officers, employees and individuals with outstanding cash advances until they have been fully settled. Also, file appropriate charges to erring individuals for failing to settle/liquidate their cash advances despite due notice.
observations.managementAction: Cash Advance amounting to ₱20,000.00 were already liquidated. However, others remained outstanding

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that management should stop hiring JO’s as security guard and instruct the Bids and Award Committee (BAC) to conduct competitive bidding for the procurement of needed security services of the City Government to adhere with the provisions of the IRR of RA 9184.

We reiterated our previous audit recommendation that Management adopt the use of Automated Teller Machine in the payment of wages of Job Order personnel in order to: 1) Strengthen the internal control system in the Treasury Department; 2) Eliminate withdrawal of large sum of money for payroll purposes; 3) Reduce the workload and time spent by the Disbursing Officer in the preparation of pay envelope for 900 personnel; and 4) Save man hours spent by each Job Order in getting their wages during office hours.
observations.managementAction: Applications for ATM of Job Orders were on process with the Land Bank of the Philippines.

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended that the City Treasurer strictly follow the provisions provided in Local Treasury Operations Manual (LTOM) and COA Circular No. 92-382 by instituting the following:

1. To acknowledge the receipt of cash and all accompanying documents by signing all copies of the Report of Collections and Deposits of Liquidating Officer instead of the Disbursing Officer.

2. The Cashbook maintained by the Disbursing Officer must be updated regularly since the City Treasurer does not maintain a Cashbook to record the daily collections and deposits for purposes of determining the balance of accountability.
 
3. To review the task perform by her staff  purposely to ascertain whether there are functions deemed incompatible that requires segregation.

We have recommended that the City Accountant, City Treasurer and the Personnel Officer to strictly comply with Section 52 of General Appropriations Act-FY 2018 to adhere with the minimum monthly take home pay.

We have recommended that the City Mayor instruct the Human Resource Management Officer to require all employees who are rendering overtime to use the biometric system for their Time In and Out in order to achieve the intended purpose of the system.  The machine generated DTR’s shall form part of the supporting documents for overtime pay. Moreover, the City Vice Mayor to stop issuing authority with no specific period and carefully evaluate the necessity of the request for OT pursuant to the conditions and circumstances enumerated in Item 3.0 of CSC DBM JC No. 1, s. 2015. Lastly, employees who rendered OT shall submit quantified accomplishment report to support their claim.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive observe the limitations provided by Section 7 of RA No. 6173 by ensuring that before hiring/ appointing these retired city officials they had already comply with the one (1) year ban to practice their respective profession. In addition, the former City Accountant and Planning Officer shall refund the amount received from the City Government during the period in which they are prohibited to practice their respective profession.

To comply with all the conditions and requirements of COA Circular No. 2016-005  for the possible request for the write-off of the specified dormant receivable accounts to the Commission on Audit ; and
Provide for Allowance for Impairment on dormant accounts to reflect its recoverable value as required under PPSAS No. 29.

We reiterated our prior year’s audit recommendation that the City Accountant should analyze and ascertain the validity of the unliquidated obligations or accounts payable which are outstanding for more than two years and if found to have no valid claims against the City, cause the reversion of the same to the unappropriated surplus after securing authority from the Sangguniang Panlungsod.

We recommended that the City Treasurer prepare the daily RCI based on the chronological sequence of checks including cancelled and spoiled checks; and eventually record the check disbursement sequentially in the same manner in the Cashbook-Cash in Bank.  The City Accountant shall also the record the disbursement based on the sequence of checks prepared as indicated in the RCI, purposely to reflect the correct balance of Cash in Bank and Expense account.

We have recommended that the approved amount of bond of Disbursing and Liquidating Officer should be increased sufficient to cover the actual maximum cash accountability for a given period in accordance with the revised schedule of bond provided by the Bureau of Treasury primarily to protect the coffers of the city.

We have recommended that the City Treasurer include checks for all bank accounts maintained by the City in the RAAF and submit the same to the Office of the Auditor as prescribed  in Section 59 of the LTOM and Section 50 of the Manual of NGAS to ensure that all checks are properly controlled and monitored as to requisition and issuance.  Furthermore, the issuance of checks must be reported chronologically in the RCI that includes cancelled or voided checks. The checks that have been cancelled or voided shall also be disclosed in the RAAF.  Finally, the management must exert effort to locate the missing disbursement voucher and it’s supporting documents under Check No. 745833 paid to National Food Authority to avoid future suspension or disallowance in audit.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive should cause the withholding of salaries and other amounts due the concerned officers, employees and individuals with outstanding cash advances until they have been fully settled. Also, file appropriate charges to erring individuals for failing to settle/liquidate their cash advances despite due notice.

We recommended that Management should stop hiring JO’s as security guard and instruct the Bids and Award Committee (BAC) to conduct competitive bidding for the procurement of needed security services of the City Government to adhere with the provisions of the IRR of RA 9184.

We reiterated our previous audit recommendation that Management adopt the use of Automated Teller Machine in the payment of wages of Job Order personnel in order to: 1) Strengthen the internal control system in the Treasury Department; 2) Eliminate withdrawal of large sum of money for payroll purposes; 3) Reduce the workload and time spent by the Disbursing Officer in the preparation of pay envelope for 900 personnel; and 4) Save man hours spent by each Job Order in getting their wages during office hours.

We have recommended that the City Treasurer strictly follow the provisions provided in Local Treasury Operations Manual (LTOM) and COA Circular No. 92-382 by instituting the following:
1.  To acknowledge the receipt of cash and all accompanying documents by signing all copies of the Report of Collections and Deposits of Liquidating Officer instead of the Disbursing Officer.
2. The Cashbook maintained by the Disbursing Officer must be updated regularly since the City Treasurer does not maintain a Cashbook to record the daily collections and deposits for purposes of determining the balance of accountability.
3. To review the task perform by her staff  purposely to ascertain whether there are functions deemed incompatible that requires segregation.

We have recommended that the City Accountant, City Treasurer and the Personnel Officer to strictly comply with Section 52 of General Appropriations Act-FY 2018 to adhere with the minimum monthly take home pay.

We have recommended that the City Mayor instruct the Human Resource Management Officer to require all employees who are rendering overtime to use the biometric system for their Time In and Out in order to achieve the intended purpose of the system.  The machine generated DTR’s shall form part of the supporting documents for overtime pay. Moreover, the City Vice Mayor to stop issuing authority with no specific period and carefully evaluate the necessity of the request for OT pursuant to the conditions and circumstances enumerated in Item 3.0 of CSC DBM JC No.1, s. 2015. Lastly, employees who rendered OT shall submit quantified accomplishment report to support their claim.

We recommended that the City Accountant and Treasurer exert utmost effort to meet the demands of timely reporting and submission of accounts.  This is to avoid undue delays in the conduct of post audit and preparing the required reports in compliance with existing laws, rules and regulations.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive observe the limitations provided by Section 7 of RA No. 6173 by ensuring that before hiring/ appointing these retired city officials they had already comply with the one (1) year ban to practice their respective profession. In addition, the former City Accountant and Planning Officer should refund the amount received from the City Government during the period in which they are prohibited to practice their respective profession.

It was recommended that the:
City Accountant prepare the necessary adjusting entry to effect the share 15% and 5% share of the OBO and DPWH, respectively from the collection of building permit;
City Treasurer strictly monitor the amount to be automatically retained with the City to be used for OBO’s programs and projects and the amount to be remitted to BTr representing the 5% share of DPWH; and
City Engineering Office as the OBO to prepare Program of Work detailing the manner on the utilization of the 15% share from the collection of building permits, and properly observe the guideline in the preparation of the program of works.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended the following:
Stop designating JOs who rendered administrative works due to existence of regular employees in the agency who is capable and available of doing such work functions and responsibilities.
Require all JOs to enrol in the biometric system of the City to ensure compliance with the required number of hours of work.
Supervision on the JOs should only be delegated to regular employees of the City.
Instruct the City Human Resource Management Officer to strengthen the internal control system in the hiring of JOs.

We have recommended that the City Budget Officer strictly comply with the Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-1 of the NDRRMC, DBM and DILG by avoiding those non-related disaster expenditures to be charged in the LDRRM Fund.

We have recommended that the Local School Board (LSB) Committee prepare its succeeding budget based on the projects, programs and activities only allowed and listed in Item 4.0 of the Joint Circular No. 01 series of 2017 of DEPED, DBM and DILG. Likewise, the City Accountant strictly adhere with the limitations set by the Joint Circular in order to ensure that only eligible expenses are paid out of SEF.

It is recommended that the Local Development Council shall:
Revisit the identified priority development projects and verify as to the reason of non-implementation
Initiate the needed coordination, monitoring and evaluation on the implementation to assure that all unimplemented projects and programs be given immediate action and implementation; and
Strictly adhere to the guidelines in the execution of the respective responsibilities by the Local Development Council, the Executive Committee, Local Chief Executive and the Bids and Awards Committee.

It is recommended that Management:
Require the forty (40) technical consultants to submit curriculum vitae and other relevant information that would support their credentials and eligibilities in their chosen field of expertise.
Stop the practice of hiring consultants for tasks which do not require expertise and that can be performed by the regular employees.
Review their functions as stated in the Contract of Service and compare it with the actual duties and responsibilities they performed to ascertain whether or not the expected output/deliverables are being accomplished.
Identify those consultants whose scope of work overlap with the functions of the other consultants which is an indication that there is need to reduce their number in order to avoid wastage of public coffers.
Stop the hiring of Legal Consultants since the City Government have already Legal Officers to handle its legal affairs and in the absence of the approval of the OSG and COA considered unnecessary and illegal which could be subject for disallowance.

We have recommended that the City Treasurer  furnish the City Accountant a duly certified list showing the name of taxpayers and the amount due and collectible for the year as basis for recording the Real Property Tax/Special Education Tax Receivable, to establish a more accurate and reliable amount on said accounts.
We have also reiterated the previous year’s audit recommendation that the Local Chief Executive instruct the City Treasurer to exert more effort and tap the services of the City Legal Officer in the collection of taxes due and collectible by sending Notice of Delinquency to RPT Taxpayers.
Eventually, we recommended that the Local Chief Executive form a committee purposely to conduct an information dissemination regarding the importance of paying taxes promptly and the consequences of non-payment of taxes  that could be subject to the issuance of warrant of levy pursuant to provisions of Sections 258 and 260 of the Local Government Code.

We have recommended that management prepare disbursement vouchers for the succeeding expenditures under the Animal Bite. Likewise, all transactions shall be certified as to existence/availability of fund, completeness and propriety of the transactions.

We have recommended that management observe the provisions of the IRR of Republic Act 9184 in the procurement of medical supplies and other expenses under the Animal Bite Program to avoid future audit disallowance.

We have recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Accountant to observe the proper recognition of withholding taxes pursuant to Section 4 of Revenue Regulations No.12-2001 which is either at the time the obligation is accrued or paid whichever comes first. In addition, the City Accountant and Treasurer ensure the remittance of all taxes withheld during the month pursuant to existing BIR Regulations to avoid incurrence interest and surcharges imposed by the BIR.

We recommended that the Management strictly remit the compulsory contributions and loan amortizations of GSIS, PAG IBIG and PHILHEALTH on the period stipulated by the above-mentioned laws. These remittances should to take priority over payment of any and all other obligations, except salaries and wages of employees

We recommended that the management comply with the aforementioned provisions of law and regulations on the imposition of liquidated damages for all contracts or projects which incurred delay or not completed on the intended completion date.

In addition, the Accounting Office should set up, maintain complete accounting records of real properties and for the General Services Officer to maintain and keep on file complete records of real properties. Both offices should also reconcile their records.

Instruct the City Accountant to de-recognize from books of accounts the cost or value of the properties that had been transferred to the required beneficiaries in order that the PPE account would be correctly and reliably presented in the financial statements.

We recommended that management should require the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) to submit the lacking bidding documents together with the copies of perfected contracts to the Auditor within the prescribed period to avoid suspension in audit of the transaction.

We have reiterated the team’s previous year audit recommendation that the Local Chief Executive should grant cash advances strictly in accordance with COA Circular No. 97-002 and to cause the withholding of salaries and other amounts due the concerned officers and employees with outstanding cash advances until they have been fully settled. File appropriate charges to erring officials for failing to settle/liquidate their cash advances despite due notice. In addition, officials or employees who are retiring from the government service should be required to settle their cash advances prior to the issuance of clearance from money and property accountabilities.

We recommended the Management to:

Exert effort in verifying the status of transferred funds and identify the unutilized balances of completed projects so that the same could be returned to source agencies or to the Bureau of Treasury  pursuant to existing government laws, rules and regulations, if the funds will no longer be used; and
Monitor the implementation of projects for which the fund transfers were intended to ensure implementation and/or to take appropriate action as the case may warrant.

We reiterated our prior year’s audit recommendation that the City Accountant should analyze and ascertain the validity of the unliquidated obligations or accounts payable which are outstanding for more than two years and if found to have no valid claims against the City, cause the reversion of the same to the unappropriated surplus after securing authority from the Sangguniang Panlungsod.

We recommended that the City Accountant should prepare and/or submit the monthly Bank Reconciliation Statements within the reglementary period, pursuant to COA Circular No. 96-011. We recommended that the City Treasurer submit the Report of Checks Issued, together with the vouchers and its supporting documents, daily to the City Accounting Office to record the entries in the Journals on a daily basis and to prevent delay in the preparation of the financial statements.
Likewise, we also recommended that the City Accountant submit paid vouchers, together with its supporting documents, and monthly trial balances to the Audit Team within the prescribed period pursuant to COA Circular No. 2009-006 to facilitate timely verification of the accounts and early detection of any deficiencies and to avoid suspension of payment of the accountant’s salaries until requirements of the Commission have been complied in accordance with Section 122, paragraph 2 of PD No. 14445.

It is recommended that the Local Development Council shall:
Revisit the identified priority development projects and verify as to the reason of non-implementation

Strictly adhere to the guidelines in the execution of the respective responsibilities by the Local Development Council, the Executive Committee, Local Chief Executive and the Bids and Awards Committee.

We recommended that the City Mayor together with other City Officials should prioritize and fast track compliance with the provisions of the Ecological Solid Waste Management Act to ensure the protection of public health and environment. In addition, the Officer-in-Charge, Waste Management Division should instruct the twenty one (21) barangays to maintain a Material Recovery Facility (MRF) which help in the segregation and preparation of recyclable materials for marketing to end-user manufacturers.

We recommended that the City Mayor should require the City Budget Officer to appropriate at least one percent (1%) of their IRA for LCPC and provide program, projects and activities for the promotion of the rights and welfare of children as required under DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2012-120.

We have reiterated our previous year’s audit recommendation that the Local Chief Executive instruct the City Treasurer to exert more effort and tap the services of the City Legal Officer in the collection of taxes due and collectible by sending Notice of Delinquency to RPT Taxpayers.

However, the City Treasurer’s Office should be advised to cause the immediate remittance of taxes withheld within the prescribed period to enable the government to allocate resources to priority development programs. We recommended that the Local Chief Executive should direct the GAD Focal Person and the City Budget Officer to prepare and submit the GAD Plan and Budget Report within the prescribed period and to submit the GAD Accomplishment Report with all the supporting GAD related reports enumerated in PCW-NEDA-DBM Joint Circular No. 2012-01 to facilitate the evaluation of the City’s performance in addressing gender issues and concerns.

Management should also require the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) to submit copies of perfected contracts and all its supporting documents to the Auditor within the period prescribed in COA Circular No. 2009-001 and Revised RA 9184.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive should direct the Bids and Awards Committee and the General Services Officer to procure commonly used supplies and equipment from the PS-DBM in conformity with the above cited Administrative Order and to support the government’s policy for transparency in government transactions and efficiency in procurement processes.

We recommended that the City Accounting Department should ensure completeness and propriety of supporting documents such as, certification that the official/employee did not use government vehicle and is not assigned with any government vehicle to establish the validity of the claims for the payment of Transportation Allowance. Proportionate reduction of Transportation Allowance claim should be made to those officials who were issued/assigned with service vehicles or those who use or availed of the vehicle issued to their office.
We recommended that the city government and the Registrar of the Registry of Deeds should submit justification why the payment of honoraria should not be disallowed in audit.

We have recommended that the City Accountant, City Treasurer and the HRMO should properly monitor and initiate the necessary controls on the salary deductions of the city officials and employees by ensuring that it will not reduce their monthly net take home pay lower than the required amount provided in the General Provisions of the General Appropriations Act.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive should  grant cash advances strictly in accordance with COA Circular No. 97-002 and to cause the withholding of salaries and other amounts due the concerned officers and employees with outstanding cash advances until they have been fully settled. File appropriate charges to erring officials for failing to settle/liquidate their cash advances despite due notice.In addition, officials or employees who are retiring from the government service should be required to settle their cash advances prior to the issuance of clearance from money and property accountabilities.

We have recommended that the Local Disaster Risk Reduction Management Council (LDRRMC) of the City should annually prepare and update the Local Disaster Risk Reduction Management Fund Investment Plan (LDRRMFIP) in order to properly monitor the utilization of LDRRM funds and ensure that programs, projects and activities were implemented in accordance with the plan.
We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive should instruct the City Accountant and City Treasurer to transfer the unexpended DRRM funds to the Trust Fund bank account in compliance with COA Circular No. 2014-009 dated August 28, 2014.

We recommended that the Accountant should be requiredto certify that the supporting documents of DVs are complete before they are being paid by the Cashier or City Treasurer.  Submit to COA the DVs with its supporting documents enumerated in Annex “F.1” to avoid being disallowed in audit.

We recommended that Management should adhere strictly to the provision of Section 21 of COA Circular No. 92-382 dated July 3, 1992. In addition, the Accounting should carefully review the unreconciled balance and make the necessary adjusting entries as the case may be.

We recommended that these expenditures should be charged against the available funds of the Department of Education as the activities and programs were regular functions of the DepEd and not any of those specified for SEF.

We recommended that Management should require the General Services Officer to strictly observe the issuance of motor vehicles only to City Government personnel whose positions entitle them to the issuance of such vehicles.

We recommended that the City Accountant should conduct a further review and undertake necessary adjustments to clear the reciprocal accounts of possible erroneous entries and/or ommisions. Likewise, the accountant should facilitate the tracing and locating  of relevant documents to serve as basis in adjusting the possible identified reconciling items in the respective reciprocal accounts, for accurate and fair presentation of the balances of Due from Other Funds and Due to Other Funds accounts in the financial statements.

We recommended that the City Mayor together with other City Officials should prioritize and fast track compliance with the provisions of the Ecological Solid Waste Management Act in order to ensure the protection of public health and environment. In addition, the Officer-in-Charge, Waste Management Division should instruct the twenty (23) barangays to maintain a Material Recovery Facility (MRF) which help in the segregation and preparation of recyclable materials for marketing to end-user manufacturers.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive should instruct the City Treasurer to exert more effort and tap the services of the City Legal Officer in the collection of taxes due and collectible by sending Notice of Delinquency to RPT Taxpayers. Likewise, a committee should be formed to conduct an information dissemination regarding the importance of paying taxes promptly and the consequences of non-payment of taxes. Finally, management should strictly comply with the provisions of Sections 258 and 260 of the Local Government Code in the collections of delinquent taxes.

We have recommended that management should continue to collect and remit taxes due the national government. However, the City Treasurer’s Office should be advised to cause the immediate remittance of taxes withheld within the prescribed period to enable the government to allocate resources to priority development programs.

We have recommended thatmanagement should ensure the full implementation of the programs, projects and activities of GAD during the year to fully achieve the GAD mandate in accordance with the provisions of the PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-01 dated July 18, 2013.
We have recommended that the management, especially GAD Focal Person should adhere with COA Circular 2014-001 dated March 18, 2014, and that a careful examination of the Annual Gender and Development (GAD) Plan and Budget and Annual Gender and Development (GAD) Accomplishment Report should be made to reflect a reliable information.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive create an Inventory Committee and direct them to conduct a complete physical inventory taking of all property, plant and equipment of the City and to prepare and submit a reliable RPCPPE, in proper form, filled –up in all particulars and approved by all officials concerned; reconcile the amounts with the accounting and property records and effect the necessary adjustments where the error was committed to reflect the correct balances of the PPE accounts in the financial statements. Moreover, instruct the Accounting and General Services Offices to maintain an accurate, complete and updated equipment/property records.
observations.managementAction: Management commented that they will immediately implement the recommendations of the Audit Team and promised to create an inventory committee that will conduct a complete physical count of all properties of the City.
observations.reason: The Management failed to complete the physical count as of December 31, 2016 as reiterated under Observation No. 1.a.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive request the SangguniangPanlungsod to pass a resolution donating the transferred properties to the recipient agencies as basis by the Accounting Office to derecognize the subject transferred properties from the books of accounts of the City. Likewise, it is recommended that the concerned recipient agencies be required to submit to the City Government copies of the duly accomplished Invoice and Receipt of Property and copies of JEVs in taking up the subject properties in their respective books.
observations.managementAction: The management decided not to transfer these lands to SEF. The management under MBTF administration will grant in usufruct to these government school agencies. This is a coordinated work with GSO, CTO, CMO and Legal Office. This management decision is final.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that:
The City Accountant be required to prepare a journal entry to transfer completed projects from Construction in Progress account to the appropriate completed projects account;

  In order to avoid similar observation in the future, require the Office of the City Engineer to furnish the Office of the City Accountant with a copy of the Certificate of 100% Completion and Acceptance in its supporting documents upon completion of the project for recording in the appropriate general and subsidiary ledger of asset and liability accounts. This will help in maintaining updated, relevant and useful accounting records.

Based on the adjusted data, the City Accounting Office should be instructed to compute the required depreciation on the completed projects, and accordingly to adjust the balances of the affected accounts, and

The Accounting Office should be directed to maintain Subsidiary Ledgers (SL) which contains the details or breakdown of the controlling account appearing in the General Ledger, and to reconcile the SL balances with their respective control account regularly and to keep the account in such detail as is necessary to meet the needs of the agency and at the same time be adequate to furnish the information needed by fiscal or control agencies of the government.
observations.managementAction: Management agreed with the observations and will implement the recommendations of the Audit Team. The City Accountant requested the City Engineering’s Office for information and details of the completed projects so that corresponding adjustments can be made.

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended that Management:

Create an Inventory Committee to undertake the physical count of inventory items as of June 30 and December 31 of each year; to prepare the Report of the Physical Count of Inventories (RPCI) by inventory account name and distribute the same as prescribed; to reconcile said report with the records of the City Accounting Office in accordance with Section 124, Volume I of NGAS Manual; and to submit to the Office of the Auditor the duplicate copy of the RPCI not later than July 31 and January 31 of each year for the first and second semesters respectively.

b.	Direct the City Accountant to correct the errors in recording purchases and issuances of inventories and observe the Perpetual Inventory System as method of recording inventories; and 
c.	Instruct the General Services Officer (GSO) and the City Accountant to maintain updated Stock Cards and Supplies Ledger Cards and regularly reconcile records to detect discrepancies and effect immediate corrections; and for the GSO to promptly prepare and submit the monthly RSMIs to the City Accountant for recording.
observations.managementAction: Management commented that they will comply with the recommendations of the Audit Team.

observations.managementAction: Management assured the Audit Team that they will immediately implement the recommendations of the Audit Team by complying with the provisions of the law and any other pertinent issuances which ensure that government resources are managed and mobilized judiciously in manner supportive of the development objectives of the government in promoting the welfare of the people.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Treasurer and the City Accountant should refund the utilized portion of the PDAF and remit the same, together with the unexpended/unutilized balance, to the General Fund of the National Government to be appropriated in compliance with the Supreme Court decision on PDAF.
observations.managementAction: Management commented that demand letters were already sent to those officers and employees with outstanding cash advances and that substantial amount were already liquidated.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Accountant analyze and ascertain the validity of long outstanding obligations. Those found valid should be paid immediately. On the other hand, recorded payables without valid documentation and which have been outstanding for two years or more and against which no actual claim had been filed should be, with concurrence of the Sangguniang Panlungsod, reverted to Government Equity, to clear the payable accounts. Henceforth, only obligations with proper and complete documentation should be recorded in the books of accounts.
observations.managementAction: Management agreed to strictly comply with the recommendations of the Audit Team.

Also require the CDRRMO to prepare annually the Local Disaster Risk Reduction Management Fund Investment Plan (LDRRMFIP) in the format provided in Annex A of COA Circular No. 2012-002.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Accountant determine the unexpended/unutilized balance of QRF, DRRM-MOOE, and other funds received by the City intended for supporting disaster risk reduction and management activities within the 5-year period and transfer the same to the special trust fund under the account Trust Liability-DRRM in the trust fund books before the year-end financial statements are finalized and submitted for audit.
